narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam. The model assesses the likelihood of passing the bar on the first try based on academic records, test scores, and personal details like yours. For you specifically, it estimated a 63.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which unfortunately crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed on their first attempt. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which ranks quite low compared to the typical student's mid-range performance in that area. Your Year 1 grade decile also contributed notably in the same direction, being well below the average decile most applicants achieve early on. These two academic rankings stood out as the most influential, signaling to the model a pattern of underperformance relative to peers who typically pass the bar. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income pulled the prediction toward success, as it exceeds the dataset average and often correlates with better outcomes. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, though below the typical 3.21, and your LSAT score of 28.50, lower than the average 36.70, provided some counterbalance but weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Enrolling as a full-time student, like the vast majority (over 92%) in the dataset, offered a small favorable nudge as well. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows stronger academic deciles pulling you ahead, but your lower ones here align more with those predicted to struggle. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework, or exploring part-time programs or alternative law schools with different admission criteria to build a stronger path forward. We're rooting for your success.
